next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 8

The house was worth four hundred thousand, maybe four-fifty if a buyer came in from the Cities, saw the water first, and didn't clock the soft board by the grill or the way the refrigerator sounded like it was breathing through a wet towel. Cliff had been pricing it in his head since Thursday because he priced everything. He noticed the stain worn off the deck rails, the screen door that no longer sat square in its frame, the cloudy lower corners of the lake-facing windows, and the old dock bolts starting to bloom orange. None of that killed the number. It just shaved it down around the edges. Martin Kessler's lake house on Elbow Lake was still worth enough to change somebody's month, maybe somebody's year.

It was not worth what Cliff needed.

What Cliff needed was forty thousand dollars.

The number had been living in his chest since March of last year, a low infection that flared every time he opened his laptop or checked his bank balance or drove past the duplex on Dale Street that had eaten his capital and his partnership with Martin and most of his ability to sleep through the night. Forty thousand dollars. Not forty thousand in the abstract, the way people said "forty thousand" when they meant "a lot." Forty thousand specific dollars that Martin Kessler had owed him for twenty-three months from a joint deal on a Roseville duplex that had gone sideways the way only real estate went sideways: slowly, expensively, with both partners telling different stories about whose idea the purchase had been.

Martin had brought the lead. Cliff had put up the capital....
open_hooks_to_carry: Doug's medication routine — the antidepressant, the anti-anxiety med, the act of hiding bottles
His tampering was the most innocent: removing prescription bottles out of pure shame, not guilt
Doug's interview with Mallick: monosyllables that create more suspicion than Nate's verbal flood
A moment alone on the dock — Doug watching the lake, the comedy becoming gentler
Doug's self-deprecating humor should be genuine and warm, not a cry for help
prev_tail_excerpt:
...n's phone. Five minutes of context, gone in one sentence.

"It was a fantasy football voicemail," Nate said. His voice was smaller now. The performance was over. The pacing, the hand gestures, the build. The sentences were short and had nowhere to go. "Three minutes. Just me being an idiot about his picks. That's all it was."

Mallick closed her notebook. Not abruptly. She closed it the way you'd set down a book at the end of a chapter, the stopping point already chosen. She took off her reading glasses and let them hang on the beaded chain.

"Well," she said. "I really do appreciate you telling me that, Nate. Thank you."

She used his first name. She hadn't used his first name before. He wasn't sure whether that was better or worse and he couldn't ask because asking would also become information, and everything he said to this woman became information, and the only way to stop giving her information was to stop talking, and stopping was the one thing he had never learned to do.

"I think we might need to follow up on a few things," she said. She stood and took her mug to the sink. "Oh, can I warm you up some coffee? Shoot, I did turn it off. I'm sorry about that. I always do that."

Nate sat at the kitchen table and looked at the foil-covered casserole on the counter and the draft board still hanging on the great room wall.

His phone pressed against his thigh. He still hadn't answered Mia's text. Through the kitchen window the lake was going dark, the amber light pulling off the surface, and from somewhere down the hall came the scratch of Mallick's pen starting up again.
secondary_character_beats: Doug's observation of the other men should be sharp and specific — his silence makes him the group's best witness, which is ironic given he saw the least
state_deltas_required: Doug's depression, divorce, and the shame of being medicated in front of his oldest friends
Doug becomes the first character the reader sees as fully human outside the comedy — his vulnerability makes the later tonal break land
